series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/4] mm: Move demotion related functions in memory-tiers.c
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves functions related to demoting memory usage in the Linux kernel from mm/vmscan.c to mm/memory-tiers.c, as per Alexandre Ghiti's suggestion. The goal is to centralize these functions in a single file for better organization and maintainability. No functional changes are intended by this move.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/4] mm: Rename node_get_allowed_targets() to make it more explicit
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the function `node_get_allowed_targets()` to `node_get_allowed_demotion_targets()` in the Linux kernel's memory management code. The original function name was misleading, as it actually returns tier nodes targeted during demotion, not allowed targets. This change aims to make the function name more explicit and accurate. No functional changes are intended by this patch.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/4] mm: Fix demotion gfp by clearing GFP_RECLAIM after setting GFP_TRANSHUGE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a demotion issue in the Linux kernel's memory management system by clearing GFP_RECLAIM after setting GFP_TRANSHUGE. The problem was caused by an incorrect ordering of these flags, which led to inconsistent behavior between regular and transparent huge page (THP) allocations. The fix involves simply swapping the order of operations, ensuring that GFP_RECLAIM is cleared before GFP_TRANSHUGE is set.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Andrew Morton requested separation of stable fixes from cleanups and preparation against current mainline, as well as careful changelog description of userspace-visible runtime effects.
review_comments.raw_body:
Please let's have the cc:stable fixes separated out from the cleanups,
and prepared against current -linus mainline.

Also, when proposing backportable fixes please ensure that the
changelogs carefully describe the userspace-visible runtime effects of
the bug.

Thanks.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Johannes Weiner noted that the original patch's fix inadvertently disabled direct reclaim for THP allocations, making the request unnecessarily wimpy, and suggested a clearer comment to explain GFP_TRANSHUGE's own reclaim policy.
review_comments.raw_body:
I don't think this is right.

The Fixes: did it this way to disable kswapd for THP allocations,
while still allowing the customary direct reclaim. Maybe a better
comment would have been: /* GFP_TRANSHUGE has its own reclaim policy */

After your fix, direct reclaim isn't allowed either, which makes the
request unnecessarily wimpy.

The Closes: refers to reclaim that should be avoided during demotion.
But if this path is taken during demotion it will already not recurse
into direct reclaim due to PF_MEMALLOC.

So I don't see a bug in the existing code. But maybe the comment could
be clearer.

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer David requested that fixes be moved to the top of the patchset or sent independently
review_comments.raw_body:
Also, please move fixes to the very beginning of the patch set, or
better, send them independently.

-- 
Cheers,

David

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Gregory Price noted that the GFP_RECLAIM flag is being masked and added back in multiple layers, making it difficult to reason about what the actual flags are at any given point.
review_comments.raw_body:
The bigger issue how many times we see this particular flag getting
masked and apparently added back in at multiple layers. We saw two
or three paths (some unreachable) that can twiddle RECLAIM flags in
the stack for demotion (which is in reclaim already, so do the flags
matter?).

It makes it difficult to reason about what the GFP flags actually
are at any given point.

But yeah I wasn't sure to make of this code, it could be as you
suggested just a bad comment.

~Gregory

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/4] mm: Fix demotion gfp by preserving initial gfp reclaim policy
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses a demotion gfp issue in the Linux kernel's memory management subsystem, specifically when handling huge pages and migration targets. The problem arises from the uncontrolled enabling of reclaim during demotion, which can lead to premature folio refcount drops. To fix this, the initial GFP flags are preserved by modifying the alloc_mask in htlb_modify_alloc_mask().

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential issue with the patch's approach to preserving initial GFP flags. Suggested an alternative solution using gfp_entry instead of modifying alloc_mask directly.

[PATCH v2 0/2] locking: contended_release tracepoint instrumentation

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new tracepoint called contended_release that fires on the lock holder's side when a lock is released while there are waiters. This provides additional information such as hold time estimation and the holder's stack at release time, which can be useful for debugging and performance analysis. The new tracepoint has minimal impact on performance and binary size. It was previously discussed in an RFC (Request For Comments) and this patch addresses some of the feedback received.

blocked waiters except for percpu_up_write(). We can use

through all other call sites and made the placement consistent where
possible: after release, before wake. It should be fixed in v3.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 2/2] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint

version_history.subject: [PATCH RFC 1/3] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint
[PATCH v2 2/2] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new tracepoint called contended_release, which fires when a lock is released under contention. The patch complements existing contention_begin and contention_end tracepoints by providing information from the holder's perspective. It enables correlating lock hold time under contention with waiter events by lock address. The patch introduces calls to trace_contended_release() in slowpath unlock paths of various sleepable locks, including mutex, rtmutex, semaphore, rwsem, percpu-rwsem, and RT-specific rwbase locks.

after the lock is released but before rcuwait_wake_up(). I also went
through all other call sites and made the placement consistent where
possible: after release, before wake. It should be fixed in v3.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 1/8] mm: use zone lock guard in reserve_highatomic_pageblock()

version_history.subject: [PATCH 1/8] mm: use zone lock guard in reserve_highatomic_pageblock()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch updates the reserve_highatomic_pageblock() function in the Linux kernel's page allocation code to use a zone lock guard instead of explicit lock/unlock and goto out_unlock patterns. The zone lock guard is a new feature introduced by this patch, which provides automatic scope-based cleanup when exiting the locked region. This change simplifies the code and reduces the risk of errors due to forgotten unlocks or incorrect locking orders. The patch also updates the include/linux/mmzone_lock.h file to define the zone lock guard and its attributes.

review_comments.raw_body:
guard() is cute, but this patch adds a little overhead - defconfig
page_alloc.o text increases by 32 bytes, presumably all in
reserve_highatomic_pageblock().  More instructions, larger cache
footprint.

So we're adding a little overhead to every user's Linux machine for all
time.  In return for which the developers get a little convenience and
maintainability.

Is it worth it?

[...]

That's a fair point, but as I understand Andrew's main concern, the
guard() usage becomes part of the code unconditionally, with no way to

review_comments.author: tip-bot2 Zijlstra
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that the recent cleanup patch optimized guards by reverting to a simple NULL test for destructors, and suggested applying this optimization to the zone lock guard in reserve_highatomic_pageblock() to avoid unnecessary code size increase.
review_comments.raw_body:
The following commit has been merged into the locking/core branch of tip:

Commit-ID:     2deccd5c862a0337a691bcfaa87919b4216e6103
Gitweb:        https://git.kernel.org/tip/2deccd5c862a0337a691bcfaa87919b4216e6103
Author:        Peter Zijlstra <peterz@infradead.org>
AuthorDate:    Mon, 09 Mar 2026 17:40:42 +01:00
Committer:     Peter Zijlstra <peterz@infradead.org>
CommitterDate: Mon, 16 Mar 2026 13:16:49 +01:00

cleanup: Optimize guards

Andrew reported that a guard() conversion of zone_lock increased the
code size unnecessarily.

It turns out the unconditional __GUARD_IS_ERR() is to blame. As
explored earlier [1], __GUARD_IS_ERR(), similar to IS_ERR_OR_NULL(),
generates somewhat sub-optimal code.

However, looking at things again, it is possible to avoid doing the
__GUARD_IS_ERR() unconditionally. Revert the normal destructors to a
simple NULL test and only add the IS_ERR bit to COND guards.

This cures the reported overhead; as compiled by GCC-16:

	page_alloc.o:

pre:	Total: Before=45299, After=45371, chg +0.16%
post:	Total: Before=45299, After=45026, chg -0.60%

[1] https://lkml.kernel.org/r/20250513085001.GC25891@noisy.programming.kicks-ass.net

Reported-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>
Signed-off-by: Peter Zijlstra (Intel) <peterz@infradead.org>
Tested-by: Dan Williams <dan.j.williams@intel.com>
Link: https://patch.msgid.link/20260309164516.GE606826@noisy.programming.kicks-ass.net
---
 include/linux/cleanup.h | 19 +++++++++++--------
 1 file changed, 11 insertions(+), 8 deletions(-)

diff --git a/include/linux/cleanup.h b/include/linux/cleanup.h
index dbc4162..ea95ca4 100644
--- a/include/linux/cleanup.h
+++ b/include/linux/cleanup.h
@@ -286,15 +286,18 @@ static __always_inline _type class_##_name##_constructor(_init_args)	\
 	__no_context_analysis						\
 { _type t = _init; return t; }
 
-#define EXTEND_CLASS(_name, ext, _init, _init_args...)			\
-typedef lock_##_name##_t lock_##_name##ext##_t;			\
+#define EXTEND_CLASS_COND(_name, ext, _cond, _init, _init_args...)	\
+typedef lock_##_name##_t lock_##_name##ext##_t;				\
 typedef class_##_name##_t class_##_name##ext##_t;			\
-static __always_inline void class_##_name##ext##_destructor(class_##_name##_t *p) \
-{ class_##_name##_destructor(p); }					\
+static __always_inline void class_##_name##ext##_destructor(class_##_name##_t *_T) \
+{ if (_cond) return; class_##_name##_destructor(_T); }			\
 static __always_inline class_##_name##_t class_##_name##ext##_constructor(_init_args) \
 	__no_context_analysis \
 { class_##_name##_t t = _init; return t; }
 
+#define EXTEND_CLASS(_name, ext, _init, _init_args...)			\
+	EXTEND_CLASS_COND(_name, ext, 0, _init, _init_args)
+
 #define CLASS(_name, var)						\
 	class_##_name##_t var __cleanup(class_##_name##_destructor) =	\
 		class_##_name##_constructor
@@ -394,12 +397,12 @@ static __maybe_unused const bool class_##_name##_is_conditional = _is_cond
 	__DEFINE_GUARD_LOCK_PTR(_name, _T)
 
 #define DEFINE_GUARD(_name, _type, _lock, _unlock) \
-	DEFINE_CLASS(_name, _type, if (!__GUARD_IS_ERR(_T)) { _unlock; }, ({ _lock; _T; }), _type _T); \
+	DEFINE_CLASS(_name, _type, if (_T) { _unlock; }, ({ _lock; _T; }), _type _T); \
 	DEFINE_CLASS_IS_GUARD(_name)
 
 #define DEFINE_GUARD_COND_4(_name, _ext, _lock, _cond) \
 	__DEFINE_CLASS_IS_CONDITIONAL(_name##_ext, true); \
-	EXTEND_CLASS(_name, _ext, \
+	EXTEND_CLASS_COND(_name, _ext, __GUARD_IS_ERR(*_T), \
 		     ({ void *_t = _T; int _RET = (_lock); if (_T && !(_cond)) _t = ERR_PTR(_RET); _t; }), \
 		     class_##_name##_t _T) \
 	static __always_inline void * class_##_name##_ext##_lock_ptr(class_##_name##_t *_T) \
@@ -488,7 +491,7 @@ typedef struct {							\
 static __always_inline void class_##_name##_destructor(class_##_name##_t *_T) \
 	__no_context_analysis						\
 {									\
-	if (!__GUARD_IS_ERR(_T->lock)) { _unlock; }			\
+	if (_T->lock) { _unlock; }					\
 }									\
 									\
 __DEFINE_GUARD_LOCK_PTR(_name, &_T->lock)
@@ -565,7 +568,7 @@ __DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_0(_name, _lock)
 
 #define DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_1_COND_4(_name, _ext, _lock, _cond)		\
 	__DEFINE_CLASS_IS_CONDITIONAL(_name##_ext, true);		\

On Fri, 2026-03-13 at 09:39 +1100, NeilBrown wrote:

> > > > 
> > 
> > I have to wonder if this is a QEMU bug too:
> > 
> > Why is it opening a file read-only and then taking out an exclusive
> > lock on it? What's the point of denying access to other readers?
> 
> It turns out that I mis-diagnosed the problem.  i.e. I guess wrong as to
> what weird thing qemu is doing.
> 
> qemu isn't using flock().  It is using fcntl() locking but at this point
> isn't trying to GET a lock, it is testing if a lock already exists.
> i.e. F_GETLK or F_OFD_GETLK.
> 
> F_GETLK doesn't require WRITE access, even when getting an exclusive
> lock.
> But NFSD does :-)
> 
> So maybe this is the fix that we want.
> 
> diff --git a/fs/lockd/svclock.c b/fs/lockd/svclock.c
> index 255a847ca0b6..67234686ef8c 100644
> --- a/fs/lockd/svclock.c
> +++ b/fs/lockd/svclock.c
> @@ -632,7 +632,7 @@ nlmsvc_testlock(struct svc_rqst *rqstp, struct nlm_file *file,
>  		goto out;
>  	}
>  
> -	mode = lock_to_openmode(&lock->fl);
> +	mode = O_RDONLY;
>  	locks_init_lock(&conflock->fl);
>  	/* vfs_test_lock only uses start, end, and owner, but tests flc_file */
>  	conflock->fl.c.flc_file = lock->fl.c.flc_file;
> 
> 
> 
> ????
> NeilBrown

Oh! That makes much more sense.

We definitely allow F_GETLK requests on local files when the task
doesn't have write access to the file, so I don't see any issue with
allowing it here. Your fix seems sensible to me.

Looking back, it looks like this may have been broken back in 2021 in:

    7f024fcd5c97 ("Keep read and write fds with each nlm_file")

?

Cheers,
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: NeilBrown
review_comments.summary: Clarified that qemu is using fcntl() locking without write access, and suggested a potential fix by changing the mode in nfsd_permission(). Provided a diff for the proposed change.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, 12 Mar 2026, Jeff Layton wrote:
> On Fri, 2026-02-27 at 10:54 +0100, Thorsten Leemhuis wrote:
> > > This was discovered on the Debian openQA infrastructure server when 
> > > upgrading kernel from v6.12.48 to later v6.12.y where worker hosts (with 
> > > any earlier or later kernel version) pass NFSv3 mounted ISO images to 
> > > qemu-system-x86_64 and it reports:
> > > 
> > > !!! : qemu-system-x86_64: -device 
> > > scsi-cd,id=cd0-device,drive=cd0-overlay0,serial=cd0: Failed to get 
> > > "consistent read" lock: No locks available
> > > QEMU: Is another process using the image 
> > > [/var/lib/openqa/pool/2/20260223-1-debian-testing-amd64-netinst.iso]?
> > > 
> 
> I have to wonder if this is a QEMU bug too:
> 
> Why is it opening a file read-only and then taking out an exclusive
> lock on it? What's the point of denying access to other readers?

It turns out that I mis-diagnosed the problem.  i.e. I guess wrong as to
what weird thing qemu is doing.

qemu isn't using flock().  It is using fcntl() locking but at this point
isn't trying to GET a lock, it is testing if a lock already exists.
i.e. F_GETLK or F_OFD_GETLK.

F_GETLK doesn't require WRITE access, even when getting an exclusive
lock.
But NFSD does :-)

So maybe this is the fix that we want.

diff --git a/fs/lockd/svclock.c b/fs/lockd/svclock.c
index 255a847ca0b6..67234686ef8c 100644
--- a/fs/lockd/svclock.c
+++ b/fs/lockd/svclock.c
@@ -632,7 +632,7 @@ nlmsvc_testlock(struct svc_rqst *rqstp, struct nlm_file *file,
 		goto out;
 	}
 
-	mode = lock_to_openmode(&lock->fl);
+	mode = O_RDONLY;
 	locks_init_lock(&conflock->fl);

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: Regression: Missing check in nfsd_permission() causes -ENOLCK No locks available
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: A missing check in nfsd_permission() causes a -ENOLCK error due to qemu's use of fcntl() locking without write access.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: Raised a question about qemu's behavior, wondering if it might be related to the issue. Did not provide any specific technical concerns or suggestions.

review_comments.author: NeilBrown
review_comments.summary: Clarified that qemu is using fcntl() locking without write access, and suggested a potential fix by changing the mode in nfsd_permission(). Provided a diff for the proposed change.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, 12 Mar 2026, Jeff Layton wrote:
> On Fri, 2026-02-27 at 10:54 +0100, Thorsten Leemhuis wrote:
> > > This was discovered on the Debian openQA infrastructure server when 
> > > upgrading kernel from v6.12.48 to later v6.12.y where worker hosts (with 
> > > any earlier or later kernel version) pass NFSv3 mounted ISO images to 
> > > qemu-system-x86_64 and it reports:
> > > 
> > > !!! : qemu-system-x86_64: -device 
> > > scsi-cd,id=cd0-device,drive=cd0-overlay0,serial=cd0: Failed to get 
> > > "consistent read" lock: No locks available
> > > QEMU: Is another process using the image 
> > > [/var/lib/openqa/pool/2/20260223-1-debian-testing-amd64-netinst.iso]?
> > > 
> 
> I have to wonder if this is a QEMU bug too:
> 
> Why is it opening a file read-only and then taking out an exclusive
> lock on it? What's the point of denying access to other readers?

It turns out that I mis-diagnosed the problem.  i.e. I guess wrong as to
what weird thing qemu is doing.

qemu isn't using flock().  It is using fcntl() locking but at this point
isn't trying to GET a lock, it is testing if a lock already exists.
i.e. F_GETLK or F_OFD_GETLK.

F_GETLK doesn't require WRITE access, even when getting an exclusive
lock.
But NFSD does :-)

So maybe this is the fix that we want.

diff --git a/fs/lockd/svclock.c b/fs/lockd/svclock.c
index 255a847ca0b6..67234686ef8c 100644
--- a/fs/lockd/svclock.c
+++ b/fs/lockd/svclock.c
@@ -632,7 +632,7 @@ nlmsvc_testlock(struct svc_rqst *rqstp, struct nlm_file *file,
 		goto out;
 	}
 
-	mode = lock_to_openmode(&lock->fl);
+	mode = O_RDONLY;
 	locks_init_lock(&conflock->fl);
 	/* vfs_test_lock only uses start, end, and owner, but tests flc_file */
 	conflock->fl.c.flc_file = lock->fl.c.flc_file;



????
NeilBrown

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 1/7] mm: list_lru: lock_list_lru_of_memcg() cannot return NULL if !skip_empty
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes unnecessary NULL checks in the list_lru_add and list_lru_del functions, as acquiring the lock for these operations will not fail.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch with a Reviewed-by tag, indicating that it meets kernel coding standards and is ready for merge.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/7] mm: list_lru: deduplicate unlock_list_lru()
series_items.patch_summary: The patch consolidates the implementation of unlock_list_lru() in mm/list_lru.c, removing duplicate code for MEMCG and non-MEMCG variants.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Applied a Reviewed-by tag to indicate approval of the patch.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 3/7] mm: list_lru: move list dead check to lock_list_lru_of_memcg()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the list dead check from lock_list_lru() to its caller, specifically in the MEMCG variant of lock_list_lru(). This change is part of a series aimed at unifying the lock_list_lru() variants. The patch author argues that only the MEMCG variant needs to check for races with cgroup deletion and list reparenting.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Reviewed the patch and noted that a comment about reparenting is already present in the code. Provided a Reviewed-by tag.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 4/7] mm: list_lru: deduplicate lock_list_lru()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch deduplicates the lock_list_lru() function by sharing the code between MEMCG and !MEMCG paths, reducing redundancy in the list_lru.c file.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Provided a Reviewed-by tag, indicating they have reviewed the patch and are satisfied with its quality.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 5/7] mm: list_lru: introduce caller locking for additions and deletions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces caller locking for additions and deletions in the list_lru API, allowing callers to synchronize auxiliary state with LRU state. The THP shrinker is an example use case where this feature is necessary to keep PG_partially_mapped and vmstats consistent. Normal and irqsafe locking primitives are provided as well as caller-locked variants of addition and deletion functions. This change enables the THP shrinker to switch from its custom LRU to list_lru, improving code consistency and reducing complexity.

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer David (Arm) suggested improving the lock_list_lru() function by adding a /*skip=empty=*/true flag and making the bool parameters more consistent, but noted that it's an internal function so not urgent.

Reviewer David pointed out a typo in the documentation of list_lru_lock_irqsave, suggesting that 'false' should be replaced with 'NULL'.

review_comments.raw_body:

Like we do in other code. But I guess we should do it consistently then
(or better add some proper flags).

Anyhow, something that could be cleaned up later.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 6/7] mm: list_lru: introduce memcg_list_lru_alloc_folio()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new helper function, memcg_list_lru_alloc_folio(), which allocates list_lru heads for a given folio under memory cgroup (memcg) control. The function is designed to be lightweight and efficient, allowing it to operate under RCU protection without pinning the memcg. This change is part of a larger effort to switch the THP shrinker to use list_lru.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Suggested renaming the function to better reflect its purpose and proposed two alternative names. Expressed approval of the patch with a 'LGTM' comment, but noted that their understanding of memcg lifetimes is limited.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 7/7] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series switches the deferred split shrinker to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists and streamlines list operations and reclaim walks.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: The deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion and list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists. The patch series converts the deferred split queue to use list_lru.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series switches the THP (Transparent Huge Pages) shrinker to use the list_lru data structure instead of its current implementation. The motivation for this change is to address scalability issues in the original implementation, which involved a complex sequence of function calls. The new approach uses the list_lru API to manage the LRU state and allows for more fine-grained control over locking. The patches also include various cleanups and refactors to make the code easier to review and maintain.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a technical issue with the deferred_split_lru, specifically that list_lru_lock() is being called on a nid+memcg combination without allocated list_lru heads, and agreed that more thought is needed to resolve this.
sentiment_signals: agreed that more thought is needed
review_comments.raw_body:
File pages aren't on the deferred_split_lru. We're calling
list_lru_lock() on a nid+memcg combination that doesn't have list_lru
heads allocated. This should either fail gracefully or needs page type
filtering in __folio_freeze_and_split_unmapped(). Needs more thought.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch updates the deferred split shrinker to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists. This change improves handling of cgroups on NUMA systems by avoiding splitting large pages across nodes. The patch also streamlines list operations and reclaim walks, and adds calls to create node heads for cgroups when splittable pages are created. Additionally, it introduces helpers to the list_lru API for caller-side locking. The deferred split queue lock is replaced with a more efficient approach using list_lru's locking mechanisms.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Usama Arif noted that folio_memcg() is called without holding the RCU read lock, which could lead to a lock ordering violation due to its dependency on obj_cgroup_memcg(), and suggested adding an rcu_read_lock() around this call.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hello Johannes!

I think we need folio_memcg() to be under rcu_read_lock()? 
folio_memcg() calls obj_cgroup_memcg() which has lockdep_assert_once(rcu_read_lock_held()).

folio_split_queue_lock() wraps split_queue_lock() under rcu_read_lock() so wasnt an issue.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the use of list_lru_lock() in an RCU read-side critical section, explained that interrupts are disabled and thus it's safe to use the plain lock, and suggested adding a VM_WARN_ON(!irqs_disabled()) at the top.
review_comments.raw_body:
In this case, the caller has interrupts disabled, which implies an RCU
read-side critical section.

That's also why it's using the plain list_lru_lock(), not the irq
variant. Same for the lruvec lock a few lines down. I followed the
example of none of that being documented, either ;)

How do folks feel about a VM_WARN_ON(!irqs_disabled()) at the top?

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a lockdep warning and proposed two possible solutions: using rcu_read_lock_any_held() or making the existing rcu_read_lock() explicit, asking for feedback on which approach to take.
review_comments.raw_body:
Okay, lockdep actually does warn. rcu_read_lock_held() is explicit and
not appeased by implicit RCU sections.

Paul pointed me to rcu_read_lock_any_held(), which we could use in
obj_cgroup_memcg() to suppress the splat and permit implicit RCU.

Or we can make rcu_read_lock() explicit in the above code.

Any preferences for how we want to handle this in MM code?

review_comments.author: Dave Chinner
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Dave Chinner requested the patch be split into multiple patches, citing that the current single patch contains complex changes that are difficult to review individually.
review_comments.raw_body:
Can you please split this up into multiple patches (i.e. one logical
change per patch) to make it easier to review?

i.e. just from the list-lru persepective, there's multiple complex
changes in the series - locking API changes, new locking primitives,
internally locked functions exposed to callers allowing external
locking, etc. These need to be looked at individually and in
isolation so we can actually discuss the finer details, and that's
almost impossible to do when they are all smashed into one massive
patch.

-Dave.

Dave Chinner
dgc@kernel.org

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH v3] mm: migrate: requeue destination folio on deferred split queue
patches_acked.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the Linux kernel's folio migration code by re-queuing the destination folio on the deferred split queue after a successful migration. The source folio is removed from the queue, but the destination folio remains and can cause underutilized THPs to escape the shrinker. The patch records whether the source folio was on the deferred split queue and its partially mapped state before unqueuing it, and re-queues the destination folio if necessary. This ensures that fully mapped but potentially underused folios are correctly handled.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 00/11] mm/zswap, zsmalloc: Per-memcg-lruvec zswap accounting
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series introduces per-memcg-lruvec zswap accounting by moving memory-cgroup awareness to the zsmalloc layer, allowing for more accurate tracking of compressed memory usage and enabling cgroups to control where their zswapped objects live. The changes also enable future consumers to account memory statistics using a new compressed object memory accounting infrastructure. The patch series includes 11 patches that make various modifications to the zswap and zsmalloc code to achieve this goal, including introducing a new per-zspage array of objcg pointers to track per-memcg-lruvec memory usage. The changes are expected to improve performance by reducing remote access latency costs and enabling more effective reclaim when pressure is localized to a single cgroup.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about inconsistent naming conventions in the zsmalloc code by renaming zs_object_copy to zs_obj_copy, and also fixed an issue where find_alloced_obj was returning a signed int instead of an unsigned int.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the accuracy of memory consumption accounting in zswap by changing how entries are charged to the zsmalloc layer, specifically using the size_class slot they occupy instead of the compressed object's size.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the inaccuracy of node stats due to the coarse PAGE_SIZE granularity, acknowledged that only NR_ZSWAP_B can be kept accurate, and accepted the small inaccuracy as part of these changes.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing Johannes Weiner's feedback about the placement of obj_cgroup_get() and obj_cgroup_put() calls in the patch series, specifically that the reference count for the pointer should be incremented before setting zspage->objcgs[]. The author agrees to restructure the code to make it more explicit and clear.

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Yosry Ahmed noted that when pool->memcg_aware is not set, writing to zspage->objcgs[obj_idx] will access uninitialized memory and likely crash; he suggested adding a conditional statement to avoid the write if the WARN_ON_ONCE warning fires.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, Mar 11, 2026 at 12:52 PM Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> All the zsmalloc functions that operate on a zsmalloc object (encoded
> location values) are named "zs_obj_xxx", except for zs_object_copy.
>
> Rename zs_object_copy to zs_obj_copy to conform to the pattern.
> No functional changes intended.
>
> Signed-off-by: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>

Reviewed-by: Yosry Ahmed <yosry@kernel.org>

---

On Wed, Mar 11, 2026 at 12:52 PM Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> object indices, which describe the location of an object in a zspage,
> cannot be negative. To reflect this most helpers calculate and return
> these values as unsigned ints.
>
> Convert find_alloced_obj, the only function that calculates obj_idx as
> a signed int, to use an unsigned int as well.
>
> No functional change intended.
>
> Signed-off-by: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>

Reviewed-by: Yosry Ahmed <yosry@kernel.org>

---

[..]
> @@ -1216,6 +1216,11 @@ void zs_obj_write(struct zs_pool *pool, unsigned long handle,
>         class = zspage_class(pool, zspage);
>         off = offset_in_page(class->size * obj_idx);
>
> +       if (objcg) {
> +               WARN_ON_ONCE(!pool->memcg_aware);
> +               zspage->objcgs[obj_idx] = objcg;
> +       }

If pool->memcg_aware is not set the warning will fire, but the
following line will write to uninitialized memory and probably crash.
We should avoid the write if the warning fires.

Maybe:

if (objcg && !WARN_ON_ONCE(!pool->memcg_aware))
       zspage->objcgs[obj_idx] = objcg;

Not pretty, but the same pattern is followed in many places in the kernel.

> +
>         if (!ZsHugePage(zspage))
>                 off += ZS_HANDLE_SIZE;
>

---

> It's a bit awkward that 0 is valid (MEMCG_SWAP). Plus you store these
> values in every pool, even though they're always the same for all
> zswap pools.
>
> How about:
>
> /* zsmalloc.h */
> struct zs_memcg_params {
>         enum memcg_stat_item compressed;
>         enum memcg_stat_item uncompressed;
> };
> struct zs_pool *zs_create_pool(const char *name, struct zs_memcg_params *memcg_params);
>
> /* zswap.c */
> static struct zs_memcg_params zswap_memcg_params = {
>         .compressed = MEMCG_ZSWAP_B,
>         .uncompressed = MEMCG_ZSWAPPED,
> };
>
> then pass &zswap_memcg_params from zswap and NULL from zram.
>
> > @@ -2071,6 +2079,9 @@ struct zs_pool *zs_create_pool(const char *name)
> >       rwlock_init(&pool->lock);
> >       atomic_set(&pool->compaction_in_progress, 0);
> >
> > +     pool->memcg_aware = memcg_aware;
> > +     pool->compressed_stat = compressed_stat;
> > +     pool->uncompressed_stat = uncompressed_stat;
>
>         pool->memcg_params = memcg_params;
>
> And then use if (pool->memcg_params) to gate in zsmalloc.c.

I like this.

I also didn't like the suggested prototype for zs_create_pool(), and
didn't like that compressed_stat and uncompressed_stat did not have
memcg anywhere in the name. Was going to suggest adding a warning if
memcg_aware=false but the stat indices are non-zero, but your
suggestion is so much cleaner.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 03/11] mm/zsmalloc: Introduce conditional memcg awareness to zs_pool
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces conditional memory cgroup (memcg) awareness to the zsmalloc pool, allowing individual pools to be configured as memcg-aware or not. It adds three new fields to the struct zs_pool: memcg_aware, compressed_stat, and uncompressed_stat, which are used to determine whether memory should be allocated for per-compressed object accounting and what statistics to account towards. The patch also updates the zram driver to create a pool that is not memcg-aware. This change aims to minimize hardcoding specific to zswap in zsmalloc by parametrizing the enum indices for compressed and uncompressed stats.

Reviewer Johannes Weiner suggested that the patch should be modified to set pool->memcg_params and use it to gate in zsmalloc.c, indicating a need for additional code changes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 08/11] mm/memcontrol: Track MEMCG_ZSWAPPED in bytes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the Linux kernel's memory control system to track MEMCG_ZSWAPPED in bytes rather than PAGE_SIZE units. This change is necessary because Zswap compresses and uncompresses data in PAGE_SIZE units, making it difficult to accurately charge each backing zspage with the correct amount of memory used by a compressed object. By tracking MEMCG_ZSWAPPED in bytes, the kernel can make sub-PAGE_SIZE granularity charging possible for this metric. The patch makes several changes to various kernel files to implement this new tracking mechanism and does not introduce any functional changes.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] mm/mempolicy: track page allocations per mempolicy

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2] mm/mempolicy: track page allocations per mempolicy

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds per-policy page allocation counters to track allocations driven by each memory policy (mempolicy) on a NUMA node. The new counters, exposed in /proc/vmstat and memory.numa_stat, follow the existing numa hit/miss/foreign style and provide detailed information about intentional vs fallback allocations. The counters are tracked per-memcg (memory control group) and per-node, allowing for more accurate investigation of pressure on a NUMA node.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the stats exposed via /proc/vmstat are guarded by CONFIG_NUMA, not CONFIG_MEMCG, and suggested using mod_node_page_state to make global counters work regardless of cgroup configuration.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hello JP!

The stats are exposed via /proc/vmstat and are guarded by CONFIG_NUMA, not
CONFIG_MEMCG. Early returning overhere would make it inaccuate. Does
it make sense to use mod_node_page_state if memcg is not available,
so that these global counters work regardless of cgroup configuration.

review_comments.author: JP (Meta) (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about inconsistent behavior when memcg is disabled, proposed an if-else statement to handle this case and update stats accordingly.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Shakeel Butt expressed concern about the number of new metrics added per-memcg, citing a significant memory cost for each additional metric.
review_comments.raw_body:
I have not looked into what these metrics mean but these are too many, at least
for the memcg. For the memcg, there is significant memory cost for each metric
added to memcg.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Shakeel Butt noted that the new page allocation counters are monotonic increasing metrics, suggesting they are more useful as a rate of change rather than their absolute value, and asked if tracepoints and BPF could be used to achieve this instead.
review_comments.raw_body:
This seems like monotonic increasing metrics and I think you don't care about
their absolute value but rather rate of change. Any reason this can not be
achieved through tracepoints and BPF combination?

review_comments.author: JP (Meta) (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged Shakeel's concern about the placement of new statistics, explaining they are intended for use alongside existing per-node reclaim stats and would be collected from the same source.
review_comments.raw_body:
We have the per-node reclaim stats (pg{steal,scan,refill}) in
nodeN/vmstat and memory.numa_stat now. The new stats in this patch would
be collected from the same source. They were meant to be used together,
so it seemed like a reasonable location. I think the advantage over
tracepoints is we get the observability on from the start and it would
be simple to extend existing programs that already read stats from the
cgroup dir files.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Shakeel Butt expressed concern that adding 18 counters for per-memcg tracking would be too costly, estimating a single counter's cost at 200KiB to MiBs on typical production machines, and suggested decoupling these stats from the rstat infrastructure to reduce overhead.
review_comments.raw_body:
Convenience is not really justifying the cost of adding 18 counters,
particularly in memcg. We can argue about adding just in system level metrics
but not for memcg.

counter_cost = nr_cpus * nr_nodes * nr_memcg * 16 (struct lruvec_stats_percpu)

On a typical prod machine, we can see 1000s of memcg, 100s of cpus and couple of
numa nodes. So, a single counter's cost can range from 200KiB to MiBs. This does
not seem like a cost we should force everyone to pay.

If you really want these per-memcg and assuming these metrics are updated in
non-performance critical path, we can try to decouple these and other reclaim
related stats from rstat infra. That would at least reduce nr_cpus factor in the
above equation to 1. Though we will need to actually evaluate the performance
for the change before committing to it.

review_comments.author: JP (Meta) (author)
review_comments.summary: Author is willing to compromise on per-cgroup granularity, suggesting a change from tracking page allocations per mempolicy per-memcg to globally per-node statistics.
review_comments.raw_body:
I could trade off the per-cgroup granularity and change these stats to
become global per-node stats.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Johannes Weiner noted that the patch does not handle the !CONFIG_MEMCG case correctly in mem_cgroup_lruvec() and mod_lruvec_state(), and suggested adding a dummy for mem_cgroup_from_task() to simplify the code.
review_comments.raw_body:
mem_cgroup_lruvec() and mod_lruvec_state() already do the right thing
for !CONFIG_MEMCG. Add a dummy for mem_cgroup_from_task() and you can
do a single, shared sequence for both configs.

review_comments.author: Vlastimil (SUSE)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Vlastimil expressed concerns that adding more counters (e.g., for preferred vs preferred_many policies) does not provide a clear benefit and may be redundant compared to tracing, requesting alternative solutions.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think I've been on of the folks on previous versions arguing against the
many counters, and one of the arguments was it they can't tell the full
story anyway (compared to e.g. tracing), but I don't think adding even more
counters is the right solution. Seems like a number of other people
responding to the thread are providing similar feedback.

For example I'm still not sure how it would help me if I knew the
hits/misses were due to a preferred vs preferred_many policy, or interleave
vs weithed interleave?

review_comments.author: JP (Meta) (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the number and complexity of policy-specific page allocation counters, proposing to simplify the implementation by using a single aggregated policy triplet (hit/miss/foreign) per policy instead of triplets for each policy.
review_comments.raw_body:
How about I change from per-policy hit/miss/foreign triplets to a single
aggregated policy triplet (i.e. just 3 new counters which account for
all policies)? They would follow the same hit/miss/foreign semantics
already proposed (visible in quoted text above). This would still
provide the otherwise missing signal of whether policy-driven
allocations to a node are intentional or fallback.

Note that I am also planning on moving the stats off of the memcg so the
3 new counters will be global per-node in response to similar feedback.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3] mm: migrate: requeue destination folio on deferred split queue

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm: migrate: requeue destination folio on deferred split queue
[PATCH v2] mm: migrate: requeue destination folio on deferred split queue
[PATCH v3] mm: migrate: requeue destination folio on deferred split queue

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the Linux kernel's folio migration code by re-queuing the destination folio on the deferred split queue after a successful migration. The source folio is removed from the queue, but the destination folio remains and can cause underutilized THPs to escape the shrinker. The patch records whether the source folio was on the deferred split queue and its partially mapped state before unqueuing it, and re-queues the destination folio if necessary. This ensures that fully mapped but potentially underused folios are correctly handled.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Gave Acked-by
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Mar 12, 2026 at 03:47:23AM -0700, Usama Arif wrote:
> During folio migration, __folio_migrate_mapping() removes the source
> folio from the deferred split queue, but the destination folio is never
> re-queued.  This causes underutilized THPs to escape the shrinker after
> NUMA migration, since they silently drop off the deferred split list.
> 
> Fix this by recording whether the source folio was on the deferred split
> queue and its partially mapped state before move_to_new_folio() unqueues
> it, and re-queuing the destination folio after a successful migration if
> it was.
> 
> By the time migrate_folio_move() runs, partially mapped folios without a
> pin have already been split by migrate_pages_batch().  So only two cases
> remain on the deferred list at this point:
>   1. Partially mapped folios with a pin (split failed).
>   2. Fully mapped but potentially underused folios.
> The recorded partially_mapped state is forwarded to deferred_split_folio()
> so that the destination folio is correctly re-queued in both cases.
> 
> Reported-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
> Fixes: dafff3f4c850 ("mm: split underused THPs")
> Acked-by: Zi Yan <ziy@nvidia.com>
> Acked-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
> Signed-off-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>

Acked-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

review_comments.author: SeongJae Park
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer questioned whether the patch's changes are still relevant after being merged in Linux 6.12 and suggested adding a CC to the stable mailing list if there is user impact on THP-shrinker enabled systems.

Reviewer noted that the requeueing of the destination folio on the deferred split queue is not sufficient, and suggested checking if the source folio was partially mapped before calling deferred_split_folio()
review_comments.raw_body:
Seems the commit is merged in 6.12.  And I assume the user impact on
THP-shrinker enabled systems is visible.  If so, should we Cc stable@ ?

---

Acked-by: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>


Thanks,
SJ

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the destination THPs were being removed from the deferred split queue during migration, and explained that this patch adds them back to the list after migration to meet user expectations.
review_comments.raw_body:
The original patches added THPs to deferred_list at fault/collapse, they
got removed but not added back to the list after migration.
This patch adds them to the deferred_list on migration. The user would
not expect the THPs to get removed from deferred_list on migration, so
this fixes user expectations.

I have CC-ed stable@vger.kernel.org to this email. Should I resend the patch
with CC stable in commit message?

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Andrew Morton questioned whether the patch provides a tangible benefit to userspace, suggesting that if no benefits can be identified, it may not meet the criteria for backporting.
review_comments.raw_body:
Maybe users just won't notice?

If we can't identify any benefit to userspace then I don't think this
patch meets the criteria for backporting.

review_comments.author: SeongJae Park
review_comments.summary: Reviewer SeongJae Park noted that the patch's fix may lead to underutilized THPs being split by the shrinker, resulting in users seeing less free memory than before, and requested Usama to correct his understanding.
review_comments.raw_body:
My guess of the user-visible consequence was like following.  Because THPs are
removed from the deferred_list, THP shinker cannot split the underutilized THPs
in time.  As a result, users will show less free memory than before.  I believe
I might be wrong and Usama can correct me in the case.


Thanks,
SJ

[...]

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Andrew Morton approved the patch, stating that it will be included in the next stable release and does not need to be rushed into mainline.
review_comments.raw_body:
That'll do, thanks ;)

Pasted, added cc:stable.  It's been there since 6.12 so I don't see a
need to rush this in, so I won't move this into mm-hotfixes - it'll go
into mainline for 7.1-rc1 after which -stable should pick it up.
